title: Noun Gender — el and la
summary: Every Spanish noun is either masculine or feminine, which changes the articles and adjectives around it.
intro: Unlike English, every Spanish noun has a grammatical gender: masculine or feminine. This is not about biology — a table (la mesa) is feminine and a book (el libro) is masculine.
Gender matters because the words around the noun (articles, adjectives) must match it. So it is best to learn each noun together with its article.
formula: el + masculine noun  •  la + feminine noun

sections.heading: Common endings (useful clues)
body: Endings are not a rule, but they predict gender most of the time.
bullets: -o is usually masculine: el libro, el perro, el dinero
-a is usually feminine: la casa, la mesa, la palabra
-ción, -sión, -dad, -tad are feminine: la nación, la ciudad, la libertad
-ma (from Greek) is often masculine: el problema, el tema, el programa

sections.heading: Watch out for exceptions
bullets: el día (the day) ends in -a but is masculine
la mano (the hand) ends in -o but is feminine
el mapa, el sofá — masculine despite the -a ending

mistakes.note: problema is masculine even though it ends in -a.
mano is feminine even though it ends in -o.

title: Articles — el, la, los, las / un, una
summary: Definite (the) and indefinite (a/an) articles change for gender and number in Spanish.
intro: In English there is only one definite article (the) and two indefinite ones (a/an). In Spanish, articles change to match the gender and number of the noun.
Because of this, the article tells you a lot about the noun even before you know the word.
formula: Definite: el / la / los / las  •  Indefinite: un / una / unos / unas

sections.heading: Definite articles (the)
bullets: el — masculine singular: el coche
la — feminine singular: la silla
los — masculine plural: los coches
las — feminine plural: las sillas

sections.heading: Indefinite articles (a / an / some)
bullets: un — masculine singular: un coche (a car)
una — feminine singular: una silla (a chair)
unos / unas — plural: some (unos coches, unas sillas)

sections.heading: Two special contractions
body: When el follows a or de, the words merge.
bullets: a + el = al: Voy al cine. (I go to the cinema.)
de + el = del: La puerta del coche. (The car door.)

mistakes.note: a + el always contracts to al.
Feminine nouns starting with stressed a- take el/un for sound, but stay feminine: el agua está fría.

title: Plurals — making nouns plural
summary: Add -s or -es to make a noun plural, and remember the article becomes plural too.
intro: Forming plurals in Spanish is regular and predictable. The main choice is between -s and -es, depending on the last letter of the word.
formula: ends in vowel → + s  •  ends in consonant → + es

sections.heading: The two basic rules
bullets: Ends in a vowel: add -s → libro → libros, casa → casas
Ends in a consonant: add -es → ciudad → ciudades, profesor → profesores

sections.heading: Spelling adjustments
bullets: Words ending in -z change to -ces: lápiz → lápices, luz → luces
An accent on the last syllable often disappears: nación → naciones

mistakes.note: ciudad is feminine, so the article is las.

title: Subject Pronouns — yo, tú, él…
summary: The words for I, you, he, she, we and they — often dropped because the verb already shows who acts.
intro: Subject pronouns name who does the action. The big difference from English is that Spanish usually drops them, because the verb ending already tells you the subject.
So 'Hablo español' already means 'I speak Spanish' — you do not need yo.
formula: yo, tú, él/ella/usted, nosotros/as, vosotros/as, ellos/ellas/ustedes

sections.heading: The full set
bullets: yo = I
tú = you (informal singular)
él / ella = he / she ; usted = you (formal singular)
nosotros / nosotras = we
vosotros / vosotras = you all (informal, mainly Spain)
ellos / ellas = they ; ustedes = you all

sections.heading: tú vs usted
body: Use tú with friends, family and people your age. Use usted to show respect or distance (a stranger, a boss, an elder). Usted uses the same verb form as él/ella.

examples.note: yo is optional — the -o ending already means 'I'.
usted = polite 'you', uses the él/ella verb form

mistakes.wrong: Yo soy yo voy a casa.
mistakes.note: Don't overuse yo — drop it unless you need emphasis or contrast.
tú takes its own verb form (eres), not the él form (es).

title: Present Tense — regular -ar, -er, -ir verbs
summary: Spanish verbs end in -ar, -er or -ir. Drop the ending and add a new one for each subject.
intro: Spanish verbs come in three families based on their ending: -ar (hablar), -er (comer), -ir (vivir). To conjugate, you remove the ending (the 'stem') and add a new ending that matches the subject.
The present tense covers both English 'I speak' and 'I am speaking'.
formula: stem + ending (the ending shows who does the action)

sections.heading: -ar verbs: hablar (to speak)
bullets: yo hablo, tú hablas, él/ella habla
nosotros hablamos, vosotros habláis, ellos hablan

sections.heading: -er verbs: comer (to eat)
bullets: yo como, tú comes, él/ella come
nosotros comemos, vosotros coméis, ellos comen

sections.heading: -ir verbs: vivir (to live)
bullets: yo vivo, tú vives, él/ella vive
nosotros vivimos, vosotros vivís, ellos viven

mistakes.note: Don't add 'ser' — the verb hablo already means 'I speak'.
Match the ending to the subject: nosotros → comemos.

title: Ser vs Estar — two verbs for ‘to be’
summary: Spanish splits English 'to be' into ser (permanent identity) and estar (state/location).
intro: English has one verb 'to be'. Spanish has two, and choosing wrong can change the meaning. The simplest guide: ser describes what something essentially is; estar describes how or where it is right now.
Both are irregular, so it helps to memorise their forms.
formula: ser → identity, origin, time  •  estar → location, condition, feeling

sections.heading: When to use ser
bullets: Identity / description: Soy profesor. (I am a teacher.)
Origin / nationality: Es de México.
Time and dates: Son las tres. (It's three o'clock.)
Permanent qualities: El hielo es frío.

sections.heading: When to use estar
bullets: Location: Madrid está en España.
Temporary states / feelings: Estoy cansado. (I'm tired.)
Ongoing actions (estar + -ando/-iendo): Estoy comiendo.

sections.heading: Same adjective, different meaning
bullets: ser aburrido = to be boring ; estar aburrido = to be bored
ser listo = to be clever ; estar listo = to be ready

mistakes.note: Tiredness is a temporary state → estar.

title: Tener — to have (and useful expressions)
summary: Tener means 'to have', and many feelings that use 'to be' in English use tener in Spanish.
intro: Tener (to have) is one of the most useful verbs in Spanish. It is irregular, and it appears in many fixed expressions where English would use the verb 'to be'.
For example, you don't say 'I am hungry' — you say 'I have hunger' (tengo hambre).
formula: tengo, tienes, tiene, tenemos, tenéis, tienen

sections.heading: Conjugation
bullets: yo tengo, tú tienes, él/ella tiene
nosotros tenemos, vosotros tenéis, ellos tienen

sections.heading: Expressions with tener
bullets: tener hambre / sed = to be hungry / thirsty
tener frío / calor = to be cold / hot
tener … años = to be … years old
tener miedo / razón = to be afraid / right
tener que + verb = to have to (do something)

title: Gustar — saying what you like
summary: Gustar doesn't work like 'to like' — it literally means 'to be pleasing to', so the grammar is reversed.
intro: Gustar confuses learners because it works backwards from English. It really means 'to be pleasing'. So 'I like coffee' is expressed as 'Coffee is pleasing to me' — Me gusta el café.
This means the thing you like is the subject, and you (the liker) become an indirect object pronoun (me, te, le…).
formula: (a mí) me / te / le / nos / os / les + gusta + singular  •  gustan + plural

sections.heading: The two forms you need
bullets: gusta — when one thing (or an action) is liked: Me gusta el chocolate.
gustan — when several things are liked: Me gustan los perros.
With a verb, always use gusta: Me gusta bailar.

sections.heading: The pronouns
bullets: me = to me, te = to you, le = to him/her/you (formal)
nos = to us, os = to you all, les = to them/you all

sections.heading: Adding emphasis or clarity
body: You can add 'a + person' at the start for emphasis or to make 'le/les' clear.
bullets: A mí me gusta el té. (I like tea — emphatic)
A Juan le gusta el fútbol. (Juan likes football.)

mistakes.note: You don't 'do' the liking; the coffee is pleasing to you.

title: Adjective Agreement
summary: Adjectives change their ending to match the gender and number of the noun, and usually come after it.
intro: In Spanish, adjectives agree with the noun they describe in both gender and number. They also usually go after the noun, not before it as in English.
So 'a red car' becomes un coche rojo, and 'red cars' becomes coches rojos.

sections.heading: Adjectives ending in -o
bullets: masculine singular: rojo → el coche rojo
feminine singular: roja → la casa roja
masculine plural: rojos → los coches rojos
feminine plural: rojas → las casas rojas

sections.heading: Adjectives ending in -e or a consonant
body: These don't change for gender, only for number.
bullets: grande → un libro grande, una casa grande
plural: grandes, azules, fáciles (add -s or -es)

sections.heading: Position
body: Most descriptive adjectives follow the noun. A few common ones (bueno, malo) can go before and shorten: buen, mal.
bullets: un buen amigo (a good friend) — bueno → buen before a masculine noun

mistakes.note: casa is feminine, so the adjective must be roja.
